# `UDP C->S 0x03/0x00` — Reliable/?

**Transport:** UDP  
**Direction:** C->S  
**Identifier:** `0x03/0x00`  
**Status:** verified

## Structure

C→S reliable sub-tag 0x00 — **Lua scenario reference** —
client-side notification carrying a Lua scenario file path.
Verified 2026-05-10 against all 187 retail samples (7/17
captures emit it). Body is 240 bytes fixed (heavily
zero-padded).

```
[0..3]    magic LE32             CONSTANT 0x00000009
[4]       sub_type u8            varies: 0x01, 0x02, 0x04 (3 distinct
                                  values; possibly enter/poke/leave
                                  events)
[5]       const u8               CONSTANT 0x07
[6..7]    field_size LE16        CONSTANT 0x0020 (=32) — max script
                                  filename buffer
[8..9]    flag LE16              0x4600 (varies; 0x0000 in zero
                                  payload variants)
[10..13]  char_uid LE32          player's character UID (matches
                                  CharInfo / UDPAlive char_uid)
[14..15]  zone_flag LE16         0x0001 OR 0x0000 (per-attempt
                                  semantics)
[16..17]  ascii_marker LE16      CONSTANT 0x0100 — start-of-ASCII
[18..N]   ASCII path             null-terminated path —
                                  e.g. `scripts\lua\scenario\redlight\
                                  h*tchenspiel.lua` (Hütchenspiel,
                                  German "shell game" NPC; 0xfd byte
                                  is the German ü transliteration)
[N+1..239] zero padding          unused buffer tail
```

Fully-populated sample (CREATION_LEVELING_LONG):
```
09 00 00 00 04 07 20 00 00 46 1a 7f 01 00 01 00 00 01
73 63 72 69 70 74 73 5c 6c 75 61 5c 73 63 65 6e 61 72
69 6f 5c 72 65 64 6c 69 67 68 74 5c 68 fd 74 63 68 65
6e 73 70 69 65 6c 2e 6c 75 61 00 [zero pad to 240]
                                  ↑ "scripts\lua\scenario\redlight\hütchenspiel.lua\0"
```

Zero-payload sample (most common — the [18..] region is all
zeros):
```
09 00 00 00 04 07 20 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
00 00 00 00 ... [228 more zero bytes to 240]
```

## Variants

- **Zero-payload**: dominant — [18..] entirely zero (header
  carries the event signal alone). Most of the 187 samples are
  this form.
- **Path-loaded**: 12 of 187 retail samples carry the actual
  ASCII path string. ALL path-loaded samples reference the
  SAME file — `scripts\lua\scenario\redlight\hütchenspiel.lua`.

The `sub_type` byte at [4] takes 3 values:
- `0x01`: most common (default form)
- `0x02`: ~30% of samples (alternate form)
- `0x04`: ~10% of samples (rare form)

These likely correspond to enter / interact / leave events for
the same scripted NPC.

## Observed contexts

Top markers:
- `IN_SUBWAY_CAR` × 8
- `POKE_START_AFTER_BUYING_GEL` × 8
- `TALKED_NPC2_CLICKDIALOG1` × 8

The Hütchenspiel ("Hütchenspiel") is a Plaza red-light district
NPC running a shell-game gambling dialog. The 187 emissions
across 7 captures correlate with sustained interaction with
this specific NPC.

The 87-emission count in `RETAIL_VEHICLE_DRONE` suggests this
also fires during vehicle/drone control activations (possibly
because the same scripts.lua scenario backs vehicle dialog).

## Open questions

- The `sub_type` enum (0x01/0x02/0x04): exact event mapping
  not pinned. Likely (start, click_dialog, end) of NPC
  interaction.
- Why does the client only ever reference the
  `hütchenspiel.lua` script in retail captures? Either:
  - This is a debug/error report channel and only this one
    script is buggy enough to trigger it, OR
  - Other scripts also emit this but our 7-capture sample is
    biased to red-light interactions.
- Is the path used server-side, or is this a one-way
  notification the server logs/ignores? (Server
  implementation absent — see below.)
- The 0x4600 flag at [8..9]: meaning unclear; correlates
  loosely with sub_type but not deterministically.

## Server-side handler

**NOT IMPLEMENTED** in Ceres-J as of 2026-05-10. No
`GamePacketReaderUDP.case 0x00:` handler in the reliable
dispatcher. Bytes are silently ignored.

Given the dominant zero-payload form, this is likely safe to
ignore in production — the client doesn't seem to expect a
response (no S→C 0x03/0x00 in retail data).

For full retail parity:
1. Add an `Action0x03Sub0x00.java` decoder that recognises
   the 240B body.
2. Optionally log the ASCII path (when present) for debugging
   client-side script issues.
3. No reply needed — fire-and-forget channel.

This is a **low-priority parity gap** since the client doesn't
appear to depend on a server response.
